data.title: OPINION: Union Budget must spell out measures to strengthen corporate earnings growth, vital to bringing back FPIs and re-energizing the market
data.description:
Union Budget Expectations
  • Union Budget must spell out measures to strengthen corporate earnings growth
  • Budget must finely balance between fiscal consolidation and capital expenditure
  • Market expects favourable tax regime and stimulus to enhance India Inc’s export competitiveness and manufacturing prowess
Did our AI summary help?

Union Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman’s Budget FY27 comes at a time when external factors weigh heavily on the economy than domestic challenges.

The most significant external challenge is the US-imposed tariffs on Indian exports. India’s repeated attempts to sign a comprehensive trade treaty with the US have reached nowhere. The additional punitive tariff of 25% is still in force. It appears that there is no visible progress in the trade negotiations, so far, due to many unresolved market-access issues. This acts as a major drag on the economy as the US accounts for one-fifth of India’s total merchandise exports. It also foments trouble for the economy in myriad ways like rupee depreciation, capital flight (FPI selloff and decline in FDI), revenue loss, dip in forex reserves, weakening of balance of payment system, loss of labour and production, stagnant exports, and many more.

The domestic market has priced in the global uncertainty, which is continuing ever since Russia’s Ukraine invasion, Israel-Palestine conflicts, simmering US-Iran tensions and the latest US–Europe standoff over Greenland. It seems the market has also priced in the near-impossibility of the trade deal in the near-to-medium term due to the US president’s love-hate relationship with India. However, the investors expect a clear fiscal roadmap from the Budget on how to proceed from here.

The Budget must also spell out measures to strengthen corporate earnings growth, which are vital in bringing back FPIs and re-energize market. For some time, valuation in the domestic market has been high compared to other emerging markets and that’s why the FPIs were continuously selling here and buying in other lucrative markets. The only way to justify this higher valuation is a handsome growth in corporate earnings, which will embolden FPI comeback.

At this juncture, adhering to fiscal glide path would be difficult for the FM. Probably, it may hover around the current level of 4.4% of the GDP. Or the government may turn its focus on the ‘debt-to-GDP’ indicator, rather than a fixed deficit target, to maintain its fiscal headroom. Fiscal consolidation is important for managing currency and fiscal stability, and crowding in private investment.

But it must be noted that aggressive tightening will adversely affect public spending and long-term growth. Also, the current fiscal year’s record capital expenditure of Rs 11.21 lakh crore has failed to enthuse private capital expenditure. It shows a deep malaise, signifying an inherent weakness in domestic demand and consumption. Thus, the Budget must finely balance between fiscal consolidation and capital expenditure.

Nobody is expecting tax sops this time. Instead, the market expects a favourable tax regime and stimulus to enhance India Inc’s export competitiveness and manufacturing prowess. However, the restructuring of GST rates, reduction in personal income tax, and special export assistance have led to a fall in the government’s tax revenue, which makes the Budget management even more challenging. To compensate this, the FM has to rely on non-tax revenues such as PSU dividends, disinvestment, RBI dividends, and auction of natural resources.

On growth front, the picture is brighter. India is one of the fastest-growing emerging economies in the world. In the current fiscal year, economy is estimated to grow above 7% despite these hurdles. Shifting global power balances and strategic requirements will force the FM to allocate more funds to defence sector. The big positive is that private defence manufacturers are set to gain from it.

We can expect more liberalization from the Budget, especially in the banking, financial services and insurance sectors as it is essential for lifting market sentiments and India’s global ambitions. Finally, the Budget’s focus would be on enhancing growth, reviving consumption and stimulating economy against the backdrop of a challenging and pessimistic global order.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

data.title: RVNL shares in focus after winning Rs 242-crore order from South Central Railway
data.description:
Rail Vikas Nigam

RVN

More

Add to Watchlist

Rail Vikas Nigam (RVNL) shares will be in focus on January 28 after the company emerged as the preferred bidder for a South Central Railway contract.

Rail Vikas Nigam said it has emerged as the lowest bidder (L1) for a South Central Railway project involving the design, supply, erection, testing and commissioning for upgradation of the existing 1×25kV OHE system to a 2×25kV system, including feeder and earthing works.

The project covers the Ongole (OGL) (inclusive)–Gudur (GDR) (inclusive) section of the Vijayawada Division under South Central Railway, spanning a total length of 154 route kilometres (RKM) or 462 track kilometres (TKM), the company said in an exchange filing.

The cost of the work is Rs 242.49 crore, and the project is to be executed over a period of 24 months.

Catch all the market action on our live blog

Related Stories

  • Mukul Agrawal’s Q3 rejig: 11 stocks exit, 3 new stocks added
  • Sensex rejig: Hindalco set for inclusion, Trent faces possible exit  

A meeting of the board of directors of the company is scheduled to be held on Thursday, February 5, 2026, to consider and approve the unaudited financial results (Standalone and Consolidated) of the company for the quarter and nine months ended 31st December, 2025.

Earlier in this month, the company emerged as the lowest bidder for a project of Rs 87.55 crore from South Eastern Railway for supply, installation and commissioning of IP based video surveillance system (VSS) in LHB coaches with 4 cameras as per RDSO Specification, Version 3.1 or latest including Rugged Hand Held Terminal (HHT)/ Tablet along with 8 TB external SSD.

In the previous trading session, the share closed at Rs 325.35, down Rs 3.85, or 1.17 percent. The share touched a 52-week high of Rs 501.55 and a 52-week low of Rs 295.25 on 01 February, 2025 and 07 April, 2025, respectively. Currently, the stock is trading 35.42 percent below its 52-week high and 9.7 percent above its 52-week low. Market capitalisation of the company stands at Rs 67,836.13 crore.

The company's subsidiary Caplin Steriles has been granted final approval from the USFDA for its abbreviated new drug application (ANDA) methylprednisolone acetate injectable suspension USP, 40mg/mL & 80mg/mL single dose vials, a generic therapeutic equivalent version of the reference listed drug (RLD) DEPO-MEDROL from Pfizer Inc., USA.

The said suspension is primarily used as an anti-inflammatory treatment in various conditions, including in allergic states, dermatologic diseases, endocrine disorders, gastrointestinal diseases, hematologic diseases, ophthalmic diseases, respiratory diseases, rheumatic disorders etc.

data.title: Asian currencies gain as dollar weakens before Fed
data.description:
Asian stocks were mixed with South Korea rising and Japan falling.
  • Asian currencies rose as the dollar hit its lowest since February 2022
  • Dollar weakness linked to US policy uncertainty and Trump's comments
  • Fed interest-rate decision and major tech earnings are key market focuses this week.
Did our AI summary help?

Asian currencies gained as the dollar sank to levels last seen four years ago as investors turned cautious toward the world’s reserve currency amid unpredictable policymaking from Washington.

The Malaysian ringgit, Thai baht and the South Korean won all appreciated in early Asian trading after the Bloomberg Dollar Spot Index slid to its lowest since February 2022 during the US session. The dollar steadied after its biggest fall since April. The greenback’s decline had accelerated after President Donald Trump said he was not concerned about the weakening.

Asian stocks were mixed with South Korea rising and Japan falling. US equity-index futures extended gains as the Wall Street Journal reported SoftBank is in talks to invest up to $30 billion more in OpenAI.

The dollar’s weakness reflects uncertainty over policy from the Trump administration, including threats to take over Greenland and comments that have raised concerns about the Federal Reserve’s independence. That forms the backdrop as the Fed announces its interest-rate decision Wednesday, just as traders turn their attention to megacap technology earnings.

“The Trump administration is taking a calculated risk,” said Win Thin, chief economist at Bank of Nassau 1982 Ltd. “Foreign exchange typically is the leader in terms of showing market discomfort with a country’s policies and economic outlook, so this dollar weakness bears watching.”

The latest decline in the dollar also follows signs of US support to boost the yen, reopening speculation around the potential for coordinated currency intervention to guide the greenback lower against key trading partners.

Reports from traders Friday indicated that the Federal Reserve Bank of New York contacted financial institutions to check on the yen’s exchange rate — a preliminary step that’s often taken before interventions.

Meanwhile, Trump’s dollar comments added to an earlier slide in the greenback, which reflected a lack of confidence in the US economy partly due to his erratic tariff policy.

Speaking to reporters in Iowa, Trump said “the dollar is doing great.” When asked if he wants to see the currency decline more, he replied that he could have it go up or down “like a yo-yo.”

In other corners of the market, gold traded slightly below its record high and silver advanced. West Texas Intermediate crude also edged up. The commodities are all priced in dollars.

Treasuries were a touch stronger with the yield on the 10-year falling one basis point to 4.23% ahead of Wednesday’s Fed meeting. The central bank is projected to halt its rate-cutting cycle as a steadier jobs market restores a degree of consensus among officials after months of growing division.

With the economy still displaying exceptional strength, the Fed’s messaging is likely to emphasize a data‑driven approach to future policy decisions, according to Chris Brigati at SWBC. Also, the tone from this week’s earnings from the so-called ‘Magnificent Seven’ tech companies should be solid.

Around a third of S&P 500 companies by market capitalization report results this week. Microsoft Corp., Meta Platforms Inc. and Tesla Inc. report earnings on Wednesday, followed by Apple Inc. on Thursday. Alphabet Inc., by far the best performer among megacaps last year, reports on Feb. 4. Results from Amazon.com Inc. land on Feb. 5 and Nvidia Corp.’s on Feb. 25.

“This week is pivotal in setting the market’s near‑term tone as 2026 progresses,” Brigati noted. “History shows that a strong January often frames the narrative for the rest of the year, with investor psychology playing an outsized role.”
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Daily Voice: Capex continuity and fiscal credibility reassure more than aggressive Budget stimulus, says Amnish Aggarwal
data.description:
Amnish Aggarwal is the Director- Research, Institutional Equities at PL Capital
  • Given global geopolitical backdrop, investors not expecting aggressive stimulus
  • Clarity on capex continuity, sectoral incentives, and fiscal credibility would be more reassuring
  • Stable, reform-oriented Budget can reinforce earnings visibility and confidence
Did our AI summary help?

According to Amnish Aggarwal, the Director- Research, Institutional Equities at PL Capital, the priority of Union Budget should be to sustain growth momentum while preserving fiscal discipline.

With inflation under control and monetary easing already in place, the Budget’s role should focus on capital expenditure, infrastructure creation, and structural reforms that promote and increase private investment, he said in an interview to Moneycontrol.

He believes a stable, reform-oriented Budget can reinforce earnings visibility and confidence, even if it does not spark a sharp near-term rally.

Amnish Aggarwal remains constructive on pharmaceutical and auto sectors. Banking and financial services continue to be a core area of conviction, he said.

If you were the Finance Minister, what would be your key priorities for the Union Budget 2026?

The priority should be to sustain growth momentum while preserving fiscal discipline. With inflation under control and monetary easing already in place, the Budget’s role should focus on capital expenditure, infrastructure creation, and structural reforms that promote and increase private investment.

Rather than headline tax changes, emphasis on manufacturing, defence, energy transition, and logistics-led productivity gains would support long-term economic resilience.

Do you believe the Union Budget can provide meaningful relief to the markets?

The Budget’s impact on markets is likely to be more directional than immediate. Given the global geopolitical backdrop, investors are not expecting aggressive stimulus. Instead, clarity on capex continuity, sectoral incentives, and fiscal credibility would be more reassuring. A stable, reform-oriented Budget can reinforce earnings visibility and confidence, even if it does not spark a sharp near-term rally.

Do you think market sell-offs driven by global events typically present the best investment opportunities?

Yes, global event-led corrections often create valuation-led opportunities, especially when domestic fundamentals remain supportive. India’s macro environment—marked by improving demand, lower inflation, and policy stability—suggests that recent volatility has been driven more by external factors than by any deterioration in local conditions. Such phases tend to reward disciplined, long-term investors focused on quality businesses.

Are you becoming more constructive on mid- and small-cap stocks amid the current market correction?

The correction has brought greater valuation discipline, particularly in the mid- and small-cap universe. While large caps are still preferred for near-term stability, selective opportunities are emerging in smaller companies with strong balance sheets, earnings visibility, and domestic demand exposure. However, the approach remains bottom-up, avoiding areas where earnings expectations remain vulnerable or valuations are still stretched.

Are you significantly bullish on the banking and financial services sector?

Banking and financial services continue to be a core area of conviction. Credit growth is improving, asset quality remains stable, and capital adequacy across major lenders is healthy. Incremental lending is increasingly driven by MSMEs, corporates, and secured retail segments. Large private banks and well-managed NBFCs are well positioned to benefit from both cyclical recovery and structural financialisation.

How do you assess the December quarter earnings reported so far? Have the results met your expectations?

December quarter results have been mixed but broadly encouraging. While demand trends were uneven across sectors, earnings growth has been supported by financials, metals, and select consumption-linked segments. Margin pressures persisted in some consumer and healthcare names, but overall corporate performance indicates resilience rather than stress, keeping medium-term earnings expectations largely intact.

Do you expect earnings surprises from steel companies in Q4 FY26, following the recent rally in metal prices?

There is scope for positive earnings momentum in Q4, driven by higher volumes and better realisations, even as input costs remain elevated. Steel companies could benefit from operating leverage if current price trends sustain. That said, earnings outcomes will remain sensitive to global demand conditions and export dynamics, making sector exposure best approached selectively.

Are you overweight on pharmaceuticals, as well as the auto and auto ancillary sectors, for 2026?

We remain constructive on both spaces. Pharmaceuticals are supported by steady domestic and global demand, even though margins may remain under pressure in the near term. Autos and auto ancillaries stand out due to strong domestic demand, premiumisation, EV-related opportunities, and export recovery, positioning them well for the 2026 earnings cycle.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Rebalancing phase, not structural damage in Nifty 50: February series opens with tactical caution
data.description:
Nifty outlook for February series
  • Accumulate selectively on declines toward 24,700–25,000 with disciplined risk management
  • Adopt sell-on-rise strategies near 25,900–26,000 unless backed by strong follow-through
  • Shift to directional trades only on confirmed range expansion
Did our AI summary help?

The January derivatives series concluded with a sharp corrective move, forcing the Nifty to retreat from the 26,300–26,400 monthly supply zone. While the price damage was swift, the internal market metrics suggest controlled unwinding rather than capitulation. Rollover participation remained resilient and open interest expanded meaningfully, highlighting that the recent decline is best viewed as a position-reset and volatility-driven rebalancing, rather than the start of a structural downtrend.

This phase has helped flush out leveraged longs, cool overheated momentum indicators, and restore balance between risk and reward—often a necessary precondition for the next directional leg.

January Rollover Snapshot – Conviction with Caution

Nifty rollovers into the February series stood at 70.75%, marginally above the 3-month average of 70.60%, but clearly below the 6-month average of 75.50%. This divergence is telling - while traders chose to carry forward positions, the reduced intensity of rollovers reflects selective conviction rather than aggressive risk-taking.

In volume terms, rollover quantities remain below medium-term averages, suggesting that participants have reduced position size while retaining directional exposure. This is typical of markets transitioning from trend-extension to consolidation, where capital preservation temporarily takes precedence over momentum chasing.

Open Interest Dynamics – Fresh Positions, Changing Market Character

Open interest expanded sharply by 15.35%, rising from 1.51 crore to 1.74 crore, the strongest open interest (OI) addition seen in the last four series. This expansion, especially during a price decline, clearly points to fresh position creation rather than passive rollovers.

The price–OI combination indicates short build-up at higher levels, particularly after repeated failures near the 26,000–26,200 zone. Importantly, the absence of panic-driven OI contraction suggests that long-term participants continue to hold core positions, while tactical shorts and hedges have gained prominence. This marks a shift in market character—from trending to tactical.

Options Market View – Supply Overhang Above, Strong Hands Active Below

The February option-chain structure paints a well-defined, institutionally controlled range:

* Heavy Call writing between 25,500 and 26,000 highlights strong supply and active hedging at higher strikes, effectively capping upside attempts.

* Peak Call OI concentration at 25,500, 25,800, and 26,000 confirms this zone as a major supply belt.

* On the downside, Put writers have displayed strong conviction near 25,000, followed by meaningful accumulation at 24,900 and 24,700, forming a layered demand base.

* Elevated Call premiums relative to Put premiums reflect a sell-on-rise and hedge-driven environment, rather than outright bearish speculation.

Overall, the options setup signals range dominance with a negative bias, where volatility is being monetized by writers and directional clarity remains deferred.

Technical Structure – Pullback into Demand, Trend Integrity Preserved

From a multi-timeframe perspective, the Nifty is undergoing a corrective phase within a broader rising structure, but without any confirmed weekly breakout. On the daily chart, the index has slipped below short-term moving averages, confirming loss of immediate momentum and rising supply pressure at higher levels. Prices are now drifting toward a high-confluence demand zone near the 200-DMA and the rising long-term trend support, where buyers are expected to defend aggressively.

On the weekly chart, price action continues to reflect range behaviour rather than trend expansion. The recent decline represents a mean reversion from the upper band of the weekly range, not a pullback to any breakout level. The index has repeatedly failed to sustain above the 26,000–26,300 zone, indicating persistent distribution near highs and lack of follow-through buying. Momentum on the weekly timeframe has cooled materially but remains above structural breakdown levels, suggesting trend fatigue rather than trend reversal.

On the monthly timeframe, the broader structure remains constructive, with prices still holding well above long-term averages. The current move appears to be a reset of momentum and positioning, allowing the market to absorb supply created near the upper end of the range.

February Series Outlook – Volatility with Structure, Patience Rewarded

The February series begins with high open interest, moderate rollovers, and a clearly bracketed options range, indicating that the market is entering a volatility-rich but directionally constrained phase. The setup favours mean reversion and tactical trading, as participants wait for a decisive resolution beyond key swing levels.

This phase is critical, as prolonged consolidation after a correction often precedes a powerful directional move once positioning becomes lopsided.

Key Monthly Levels – Swing-Based Reference Zones

* Major Resistance: 26,300–26,400 (monthly swing high & supply zone)

* Intermediate Resistance: 25,900–26,000

* Major Support: 24,700–24,500 (monthly swing low & high-conviction demand)

* Structural Support: 24,000–23,900 (long-term breakout base)

A sustained acceptance above 26,000 would indicate absorption of supply and signal trend resumption. Conversely, a decisive breakdown below 24,700 would suggest deeper corrective risk toward lower monthly supports.

Strategy Playbook – Trade Levels, Not Emotions

* Accumulate selectively on declines toward 24,700–25,000 with disciplined risk management

* Adopt sell-on-rise strategies near 25,900–26,000 unless backed by strong follow-through

* Shift to directional trades only on confirmed range expansion

Bottom Line

The February 2026 derivatives series reflects a market that is cooling excesses, not losing strength. Moderate rollovers, sharp open interest expansion, and a tightly defined options structure all point toward consolidation with underlying resilience. Until key monthly levels give way, the market rewards patience, tactical execution, and respect for ranges, setting the stage for the next high-conviction move.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

data.title: First Tick: Top global cues to watch in today’s trade
data.description:
Nifty outlook for February series

Indian benchmark indices Sensex and Nifty are likely to open higher on January 28, supported by positive cues from GIFT Nifty, which was trading higher at around 25,453.

Track the latest updates on GIFT Nifty right here on Moneycontrol.

Indian equity benchmark indices ended higher on January 27, recouping part of the losses from the previous session in a highly volatile trade, led by metal, financial, IT and oil & gas stocks. Also, the market sentiment received a modest lift following India–European Union trade agreement.

At close, the Sensex was up 319.78 points or 0.39 percent at 81,857.48, and the Nifty was up 126.75 points or 0.51 percent at 25,175.40.

Here is how financial markets across the globe fared overnight:

GIFT Nifty (Up)

Related Stories

  • Rebalancing phase, not structural damage in Nifty 50: February series opens with tactical caution
  • Budget 2026: Capital gains tax fears rattle markets as weak sentiment amplifies risk
  • Budget 2026 Playbook: 5 triggers that could ignite a post-Budget market rally

The GIFT Nifty is trading higher at around 25,453, indicating a positive start for the day.

Asian Equities (Mixed)

Asia-Pacific markets opened mostly higher on Wednesday, tracking gains on Wall Street after the S&P 500 closed at a record high.

US Equities (Mixed)

The S&P 500 barely managed a record closing high on Tuesday, its fifth straight day of gains with investor ​optimism ahead of megacap earnings reports being countered by a mixed reception to the latest earnings reports and a massive selloff in health insurer stocks.

The Dow Jones Industrial ⁠Average fell 408.99 points, or 0.83%, to 49,003.41, the S&P 500 gained 28.37 points, or 0.41%, to 6,978.60 and the Nasdaq Composite gained 215.74 points, or 0.91%, to 23,817.10.

US Bond Yield (Down)

Yields on the 10-year and 2-year US Treasuries were down marginally at 4.22% and 3.55%, respectively, in the early trade on Wednesday.

Dollar Index (Slips)

The dollar was on the ropes near multi-year lows on Wednesday after investors sold it aggressively when U.S. President Donald Trump seemed to shrug off its recent decline, while Wall Street marched on to fresh record highs.

Asian Currencies (Gain)

Asian currencies gained as the dollar sank to levels last seen four years ago as investors turned cautious toward the world’s reserve currency amid unpredictable policymaking from Washington.

Crude (Rises)

Oil held near a 16-week high after the dollar slumped, while traders also monitored US President Donald Trump’s threats against Iran.

Gold (Up)

Gold surged above $5,200 an ounce on Wednesday to a record high, extending a historic rally as economic and geopolitical uncertainty drove demand for the safe-haven yellow metal.

Fund Flow Action

On January 27, foreign institutional investors (FIIs) were net sellers in the equity market, offloading shares worth over Rs 3,000 crore, while domestic institutional investors (DIIs) provided strong support by purchasing equities worth nearly Rs 9,000 crore.

Hope you're all set for today's trade. We wish you a profitable day ahead.

data.title: Trading Plan: Can Nifty 50, Bank Nifty see follow-through buying interest?
data.description:
Nifty Trading Plan for January 28
  • If Nifty 50 breaks and sustains above 25,450, an uptrend towards 25,600 possible
  • Below 25,450, range-bound trading may continue with support at 24,900
  • Bank Nifty needs to take out 59,500 for a sustainable rally towards 60,000
  • Below 59,500, consolidation may be seen with key support at 58,100
Did our AI summary help?

The Nifty 50 and Bank Nifty rebounded nicely but need stronger momentum and technical indicators for follow-through buying interest. Until then, there could be range-bound trading for a few more sessions. If the Nifty 50 breaks and sustains above 25,450, an uptrend towards 25,600 is possible. Until then, range-bound trading may continue with support at 24,900. Meanwhile, the Bank Nifty needs to take out 59,500 for a sustainable rally towards 60,000; however, below this level, consolidation may be seen with key support at 58,100, experts said.

On January 27, the Nifty 50 jumped 127 points (0.51 percent) to 25,175, while the Bank Nifty rallied 732 points (1.25 percent) to 59,205. Market breadth remained unfavourable, as about 1,622 shares declined against 1,311 advancing shares on the NSE.

Nifty Outlook and Strategy

Ashish Kyal, CMT, Founder and CEO of Waves Strategy Advisors

Over the past 14 consecutive sessions, Nifty has continued to drift lower while protecting the prior day’s high on a closing basis. In the previous session, higher volatility was witnessed due to the monthly F&O expiry, and now markets are heading into an important and much-awaited event—the Union Budget scheduled later this week.

Related Stories

  • Budget 2026 Playbook: 5 triggers that could ignite a post-Budget market rally
  • We believe markets still need a proper 10-15% correction: UTI AMC’s Ajay Tyagi

Hence, volatility is expected to remain elevated throughout the week. On the daily chart, Nifty is currently trading near the upward-sloping trendline of the channel, and a breakdown below the 24,920 level could trigger a panic sell-off and drag prices lower towards the 24,728 level. Meanwhile, a sustainable move above 25,250 could result in short-term pullbacks.

Key Resistance: 25,500

Key Support: 24,728

Strategy: Long positions can be created if Nifty Futures give a 15-minute close above 25,250, with a stop-loss at 25,120 and targets at 25,380 followed by 25,500.

Aditya Thukral, Founder & Analyst of AT Research & Risk Managers

The Nifty 50 has been hovering around the 200-day exponential moving average for the past few days and has remained sideways within the broader range of 24,900–25,450. However, the price structure still remains negative, with the formation of lower highs and lower lows. This is the sixth trading session in which prices have closed below the 20-day, 50-day, and 100-day EMAs, all of which are now sloping downwards. A halt to this selling and a fall in volatility are only possible when the index closes above 25,450 on a daily timeframe, which is the resistance level from the falling trendline.

Technically, the short-term downtrend remains in force, and with deteriorating market breadth, selling pressure might resume, but only after completion of the corrective move. At the same time, the 14-period RSI is bouncing from the oversold zone on the daily timeframe, suggesting that a bounce can be seen in index prices at least up to the resistance area of 25,450. Positioning in the derivatives segment also suggests a sideways market in the near term.

Key Resistance: 25,450, 25,600

Key Support: 25,000, 24,800

Strategy: Buy Nifty Futures on dips around 25,200 with a stop-loss at 25,049, targeting 25,500.

Preeti K Chabra, Founder of Trade Delta

On the daily timeframe, Nifty formed a bullish candle but continues to trade within a steep downward-sloping channel, where any rebound is met with fresh selling pressure, clearly reinforcing bearish control. The index is trading below the 40-day EMA at 25,737 and the 20-day SMA (middle Bollinger Band) at 25,749, indicating sustained selling pressure at higher levels.

On the downside, 24,969 emerges as a crucial support level, coinciding with the 78.6 percent Fibonacci retracement of the rally from the September 30 low of 24,587 to the January 5 high of 26,373. A decisive breakdown below this level could accelerate further weakness. On the upside, the 61.8 percent retracement level of 25,269 is acting as an immediate resistance. The daily RSI at 34.29, although positioned below its signal line, is trending upward, suggesting some relief in selling pressure.

On the weekly timeframe, Nifty closed slightly below the 40-week EMA at 25,186, and a sustained close below this crucial level would likely resume selling pressure and deepen the corrective phase.

From a derivatives perspective, weekly options data is neutral, indicating no clear direction. Overall, the broader structure remains bearish, and unless Nifty decisively breaks out of the downward-sloping channel, the index is likely to remain under pressure with a downside bias.

Key Resistance: 25,269, 25,347

Key Support: 24,969, 24,850

Strategy: Consider selling Nifty Futures near 25,269 for a target of 24,969 followed by 24,850, with a stop-loss at 25,347.

Bank Nifty - Outlook and Positioning

Ashish Kyal, CMT, Founder and CEO of Waves Strategy Advisors

In the previous session, Bank Nifty took a breather and staged a smart recovery from the day’s low after the sharp decline witnessed over the past week. However, the index failed to close above the prior day’s high, which keeps the daily bias tilted towards the sell side. For any meaningful reversal to occur, a decisive close above the previous day’s high is essential.

On the daily chart, prices are currently hovering near the 23.6 percent retracement of the entire rally that began on September 25, 2025. Any breakdown below 58,100 could accelerate the sell-off further.

A break above 59,600 can confirm a short-term reversal, which could push prices higher towards the 59,900–60,000 zone, while a break back below 58,100 is required for selling pressure to resume.

Key Resistance: 60,000

Key Support: 58,100

Strategy: Long positions in Bank Nifty Futures can be created above 59,450, with a stop-loss at 59,150 and targets at 59,750 followed by 60,000.

Aditya Thukral, Founder & Analyst of AT Research & Risk Managers

The short-term trend of the banking index appears sideways, as the index is hovering between 58,100 and 59,600, which are the support and resistance areas derived from the 20-day EMA and the 100-day EMA, respectively. The fall from the top appears to be corrective in nature, and the index may once again test the psychological resistance of 60,000 on a break above 59,600. Considering the fact that prices are able to protect the 50-day and 100-day EMAs, there are higher chances of a possible recovery.

From the positioning in the derivatives segment, aggressive long build-up and mild short covering suggest that anticipation of limited downside risk prevails in the near term. This makes sentiment somewhat positive for the banking sector.

Key Resistance: 59,600, 60,000

Key Support: 58,600, 58,200

Strategy: Buy Bank Nifty Futures on dips around 59,300 with a stop-loss at 58,974, targeting 59,900.

Preeti K Chabra, Founder of Trade Delta

Bank Nifty formed a strong bullish candle on the daily chart and closed above the 40-day EMA at 59,190 and the 38.2 percent Fibonacci retracement level at 59,184, calculated from the rally between the November 7 low of 57,157 and the January 5 high of 60,437, thereby reinforcing a strong near-term support base. A sustained close above this level over the next few trading sessions will add bullish momentum to Bank Nifty. On the upside, the 20-day SMA (middle Bollinger Band) at 59,515 is acting as a crucial resistance.

The daily RSI at 48.5 is slightly below the signal line but is trending upward, suggesting a loss of selling pressure. From a derivatives perspective, monthly options data remains neutral, as it is the start of a new series, thus providing no clear indication.

Overall, Bank Nifty is showing signs of recovery, and traders may consider adopting a buy-on-dips strategy.

Key Resistance: 59,400, 59,515

Key Support: 58,797, 58,600

Strategy: Buy Bank Nifty Futures near the cash reference level of 58,797 for an upside target of 59,400 followed by 59,515, while maintaining a stop-loss at 58,600.

data.title: We believe markets still need a proper 10-15% correction: UTI AMC’s Ajay Tyagi
data.description:
The budget is not a big market-moving event. It is simply a statement of the government’s income and expenditure for the next twelve months., Tyagi said.
  • Markets need a 10–15% correction; mid and small caps remain expensive
  • Budget seen as a non-event; consumption recovery expected but will be gradual
  • Large caps and private sector banks preferred for better valuation comfort
Did our AI summary help?

While the market has seen some corrections over the last year, Ajay Tyagi, Senior Executive Vice President & Head – Equity, UTI AMC believes that not much has  more is needed. “What we have seen is not a correction, just a sideways drift. There have been some corrections in small caps, but that does not qualify as a real correction,” he said.

In conversation with Moneycontrol, Tyagi spoke about why he believes the budget continues to be a “non-event” for the market and why he believes that consumption could finally see the much awaited turnaround.

Edited excerpts

How are you viewing the markets currently?

Over the last one-and-a-half years, markets have effectively gone nowhere. What we have seen is not a correction, just a sideways drift. There have been some corrections in small caps, but that does not qualify as a real correction. We believe markets still need a proper 10–15% correction. Mid and small caps remain extremely expensive and need a meaningful reset.

Related Stories

  • Trading Plan: Can Nifty 50, Bank Nifty see follow-through buying interest?
  • Trade Spotlight: How should you trade LIC, Indian Hotels, Asahi India Glass, JSW Steel, MCX India, a...
  • Trade setup for January 28: Top 15 things to know before the opening bell

Is the SMID-cap space still as frothy as its peak?

The froth has come down from the September 2024 peak, but this is relative. Are we better than September 2024? Yes. Are we attractive today? The answer is an emphatic no. Valuations are still not attractive enough to bring in meaningful foreign capital.

What about earnings growth?

Earnings growth over the last two years has been disappointing. FY2025 came in at around 4%, FY2026 is tracking at roughly 7–8%, and the market is pencilling in close to 16% for FY27. FY2027 should be better than FY2026, but there is no visibility of earnings jumping from 8% to 17%. Recent results have not improved confidence.

What are the expectations of the budget for the market?

The budget is not a big market-moving event. It is simply a statement of the government’s income and expenditure for the next twelve months.  Permanent or structural decisions are no longer announced in budgets. That stopped about ten years ago. You may see some income tax tweaks, which reduce government revenue and leave a bit more money with households, which may then get spent. Beyond that, most budget announcements are incremental. Last year’s income tax cuts led to a sharp rally in consumer stocks, but if you look at the charts, most of those stocks were back to pre-budget levels within a week.

What kind of budget announcements actually matter for you as a money manager?

We only pay attention if there is something with medium-term implications. For example, a meaningful R&D tax incentive would change the earnings trajectory for pharma companies. Capital gains taxation is another area that matters. If long-term capital gains rules are meaningfully altered, that would have real market implications.

Are there still investment opportunities in the current market?

There are always ideas. Between 1997 and 2003, markets were largely flat, yet sectors like IT and banking delivered exceptional returns. So, it would be incorrect to say there are no opportunities. That said, our overall view is that markets are likely to remain range-bound to negative over the next 12–15 months. We are tilting portfolios more towards large caps where relative valuation comfort is higher. Within sectors, private sector banks stand out because in an otherwise expensive market, they are relatively inexpensive. Auto OEMs are trading slightly above long-term averages but nothing alarming. IT is also reasonably valued despite concerns around AI-led disruption. Beyond sector views, the approach remains bottom-up, looking for value even within expensive segments.

Are IPOs on your radar?

We are very selective. We try to evaluate almost every IPO that comes to the market. In a typical year, if there are around 70 IPOs, we would evaluate at least 55–60 of them, which is nearly 80–90% of the pipeline. However, we end up investing in only about 10–15% of IPOs. Our experience and the data clearly suggest that IPOs, over the long term, do not create significant wealth. Most interest is around listing gains, which is not a strategy that works well for large, long-term funds.

A common discussion in the market is the too many IPOs are hurting market performance by draining liquidity? Do you agree?

We do not look at markets through a technical or liquidity-driven lens. Our framework is fundamentally driven. If markets are trading at 25 times forward earnings when the long-term average is closer to 15, they are expensive regardless of money flows. Circular arguments around liquidity dilute conviction. History shows that money follows returns, not the other way around.

How big a concern are geopolitics and tariffs for markets right now?

Valuations alone justify caution, but geopolitics has added a sentiment drag. FII outflows are the largest seen in over three decades, despite the economy doing reasonably well and macro indicators remaining stable. This suggests factors beyond fundamentals influencing investor behaviour.

So, why are FII outflows continuing despite stable domestic fundamentals?

A big factor is US exceptionalism. Strong dollar returns in US markets have reduced the incentive for global investors to look elsewhere. This has been a headwind for emerging markets as a whole, not just India. Emerging markets are trading at a significant discount to the US, and India’s valuation premium versus other emerging markets has normalized. When flows return, India may be a beneficiary.

What is your view on consumption?

Our view on consumption is positive, and the math is very clear. Income tax cuts in the last Budget amounted to about $11 billion-worth of savings. GST rationalization in October last year added another $22 billion that is $33 billion of recurring savings every year. In a 4 trillion dollar economy, that is close to 1% of GDP. One percent of household savings on a recurring basis is a very large number.

Will this translate into an immediate consumption recovery?

Not immediately. This is a K-shaped recovery. A large part of the population lost jobs or livelihoods post-COVID and had to borrow through unsecured loans or MFIs. The first thing households will do when additional income comes in is repay debt. Consumption comes later. But this income is recurring. Once loans are paid off, discretionary spending resumes, and that is how consumption gradually picks up. The 8th Pay Commission will add further support to consumption. It could contribute at least 50 basis points on its own, and it will be recurring with arrears. When you put tax savings, GST rationalisation and the pay commission together, households are sitting on a meaningful spending kitty.

Consumption has been the most disappointing sector over the last three to four years, which is precisely why we have the most hope from it. India is a consumption-driven economy, with 60–65% of GDP coming from domestic consumption. We cannot predict exact timing, but directionally, consumption is where our optimism lies.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.
